warnings: Special risk #3 explicitly flags the franchisor's financial condition as calling into question its ability to provide support.
Special risk #4 flags short operating history.
Special risk #5 flags significant number of signed but unopened franchises.
Special risk #6 flags unregistered primary trademark.
Item 19 Table 1 covers only 4 outlets that operated the full year; Tables 2 and 3 cover additional outlets opened during 2025 — small sample sizes limit reliability.

extracted.brandBackground: Founded in 2019 and franchising since October 2022, The Vital Stretch offers one-on-one assisted regenerative stretching services in small studio formats (VS Standard and VS Non-Traditional).
extracted.conceptRationale: The brand operates assisted-stretching studios focused on health, mobility, and recovery — a fitness/wellness studio model with no food or beverage component.
extracted.staffingRationale: Item 15 requires an on-site Key Manager at all times during hours of operation, and the VSPc practitioner training requirement implies multiple certified stretch practitioners on staff.

leadership.name: Melissa Goldring
leadership.background: Director of Physical Therapy at Advanced Health Professionals since 1998; co-founded The Vital Stretch in 2019 and the franchising entity in 2022.
leadership.whyItMatters: Primary decision-maker and clinical credibility anchor for the brand.

leadership.name: Robert Goldring
leadership.background: Chiropractic Physician at Advanced Health Professionals since 1994; co-founded The Vital Stretch in 2019 and the franchising entity in 2022.
leadership.whyItMatters: Oversees operations and co-developed the proprietary VSPc training methodology.

leadership.name: Casey Stanton
leadership.background: CMO for Joshua Tree Experts and The Exercise Coach; founder of CMOx and CTOx; joined The Vital Stretch in November 2025.
leadership.whyItMatters: Leads brand and franchisee marketing strategy at a critical growth stage.

leadership.name: Torin Otis
leadership.background: Previously Director of Franchise Development at Franchise Fastlane (2022–2025); joined The Vital Stretch in June 2025.
leadership.whyItMatters: Drives franchisee recruitment and pipeline growth.

leadership.name: Kara Giangreco
leadership.background: Former manager at Orange Theory Fitness and European Wax Center; joined The Vital Stretch in May 2023.
leadership.whyItMatters: Provides on-the-ground franchisee support and operational coaching.

leadership.name: Evan Bonenfant
leadership.background: Lead Stretch Practitioner since April 2019; became Training Program Coordinator in January 2024.
leadership.whyItMatters: Owns the proprietary practitioner certification program critical to service quality.

item19.notes: Item 19 contains three tables: Table 1 covers 4 outlets open for the full 2025 calendar year (average annual gross revenue $190,291); Table 2 covers 9 outlets that opened during 2025 with monthly ramp data; Table 3 covers active member counts across all 14 franchise outlets during 2025. No profitability (EBITDA/net income) table is disclosed. Sample sizes are small and results may not be representative.

flatMonthlyFees.name: Local Marketing Spending Requirement (post-benchmark)
flatMonthlyFees.source: Item 6, p.12 — minimum after 75 stretch-hours/week benchmark met; $3,000/month before benchmark

flatMonthlyFees.name: Minimum Royalty (from month 13)
flatMonthlyFees.source: Item 6, p.11 — greater of 7% of Gross Revenue or $500/month beginning month 13

hiddenCosts.name: Annual Franchise Meeting Registration Fee
hiddenCosts.description: Mandatory annual convention attendance fee of approximately $1,000 per attendee, plus franchisee-paid travel, lodging, and meals.

hiddenCosts.name: Renewal Fee
hiddenCosts.description: One-time $10,000 fee due at renewal, plus cost of studio renovation/reimaging to current standards.

hiddenCosts.name: Transfer Fee
hiddenCosts.description: One-time $10,000 fee payable prior to any approved transfer of franchise interest.

hiddenCosts.name: On-Site Training Fee (additional/refresher)
hiddenCosts.description: $750 per day per instructor for additional or refresher on-site training beyond initial program.

hiddenCosts.name: Additional Initial Trainee Fee
hiddenCosts.description: $1,500 per person for more than 4 trainees attending the initial training program.

hiddenCosts.name: Step-in Rights Fee
hiddenCosts.description: Greater of 10% of weekly Gross Revenue or $1,500/week if franchisor takes over studio due to franchisee death or disability.

hiddenCosts.name: Audit Fee
hiddenCosts.description: All franchisor audit costs (minimum $500) if franchisee understates Gross Revenue by 5% or more.

hiddenCosts.name: Operations Manual Replacement Fee
hiddenCosts.description: $10,000 fee if franchisee requires a replacement Operations Manual or provides a copy to a third party.

hiddenCosts.name: Relocation Fee
hiddenCosts.description: $5,000 fee payable upon approval of a studio relocation request.

hiddenCosts.name: Product Testing and Supplier Evaluation Fee
hiddenCosts.description: $500 plus out-of-pocket expenses to evaluate a proposed unapproved supplier or product.

hiddenCosts.name: Insurance Procurement Administrative Fee
hiddenCosts.description: If franchisor obtains insurance on franchisee's behalf, franchisee pays premium plus 25% administrative fee.

hiddenCosts.name: Technology Development Fee (potential)
hiddenCosts.description: Up to 1% of monthly Gross Revenue for technology modifications and enhancements; not currently charged but reserved.
hiddenCosts.source: Item 6 / Franchise Agreement §4.6, p.10 of FA — noted as reserved right

hiddenCosts.name: Advertising Cooperative Contribution (if established)
hiddenCosts.description: Up to 2% of monthly Gross Revenue if franchisor establishes a local/regional advertising cooperative.

hiddenCosts.name: Grand Opening Marketing (pre-opening)
hiddenCosts.description: Minimum $2,000 in digital marketing spend during the 2 months prior to opening, plus $3,000/month for approximately 2 months post-opening as part of grand opening.

operationalRisks.title: Franchisor Financial Condition
operationalRisks.description: The franchisor's financial statements show a members' deficit of $1,283,086 and net losses of $673,268 (2025), $446,904 (2024), and $79,723 (2023), explicitly flagged in the Special Risks section as calling into question the franchisor's ability to provide support.
operationalRisks.source: Special Risks, p.v; Item 21 / Exhibit A financial statements

operationalRisks.title: Short Operating History
operationalRisks.description: The franchisor was formed in July 2022 and has only offered franchises since October 2022, making this a very early-stage system with limited track record.

operationalRisks.title: Significant Unopened Franchises
operationalRisks.description: As of December 31, 2025, 11 franchise agreements were signed but studios not yet opened, indicating potential delays and execution risk across the system.

operationalRisks.title: Unregistered Primary Trademark
operationalRisks.description: The primary trademark 'The Vital Stretch' is registered (Reg. No. 5,414,633) but the Special Risks section warns that the primary trademark used in the business is not federally registered, creating potential brand identity risk.

operationalRisks.title: Mandatory Minimum Royalty and Marketing Payments
operationalRisks.description: Franchisees must pay minimum royalties ($500/month from month 13) and minimum local marketing spend ($1,500–$3,000/month) regardless of revenue, with termination risk for non-compliance.

operationalRisks.title: Out-of-State Dispute Resolution
operationalRisks.description: All disputes must be arbitrated or litigated in Norwalk, Connecticut (franchisor's home county), which may disadvantage franchisees located in other states.

operationalRisks.title: Supplier Control and Pricing Risk
operationalRisks.description: Franchisees must purchase 80–90% of equipment, inventory, and services from franchisor-designated suppliers at prices set by those suppliers, which may exceed open-market prices.

operationalRisks.title: Franchisor Right to Modify System
operationalRisks.description: Franchisor may change the Operations Manual, system standards, and required products/services at any time, potentially requiring franchisees to make additional investments.

operationalRisks.title: Non-Compete Covenant Post-Termination
operationalRisks.description: A 2-year, 20-mile radius non-compete applies after termination or expiration, restricting franchisee's ability to operate in the same industry.

operationalRisks.title: Termination Without Cure for Multiple Defaults
operationalRisks.description: Franchisor may terminate immediately (no cure period) for 20 enumerated non-curable defaults including 3 written notices within 12 months, bankruptcy, or abandonment.

rentDetail.source: Item 7, p.16-18 — rent (2 months) low estimate $5,000 / high $14,000 for VS Standard (1,000-1,200 sq ft); per-month figure implied by 2-month deposit range

riskReasons: 3 high-severity operational tripwires disclosed — see the tripwires section.
Franchisor financial distress disclosed (negative net worth) — confirm its ability to support the system against the audited statements.
notes: Average rent not found; fixed costs may be understated.

underwriting.assessment: $250,000 liquid covers the $207,750 mid-point build-out without financing, though that leaves little working-capital cushion. At mid-cohort revenue, modeled cash flow after debt service is about $11,181/mo before payroll and maintenance — a thin margin of error.

insights.conceptRationale: The brand operates assisted-stretching studios focused on health, mobility, and recovery — a fitness/wellness studio model with no food or beverage component.

benchmark.label: Boutique fitness / studio
benchmark.dominantRisk: Member retention/churn and fixed-cost coverage — NOT cost of goods. The deal is decided by recurring membership against rent and equipment lease.
benchmark.rampNote: 9–18 months to a mature, retained member base.
considerations: COGS is trivial; the risks are member churn and covering fixed rent + equipment lease.
Ask Item 20 operators for monthly retention, average membership lifetime, and break-even member count.
Model CAC and free-trial conversion — member acquisition cost is the hidden operating line.

crossCheck.message: No operating-EBITDA margin is disclosed in Item 19. The 15–28% band below is an industry estimate for this concept — validate it against real franchisee P&Ls (Item 20) before relying on any margin.

buildup.label: − Occupancy / rent (not disclosed)
buildup.note: Item 7 disclosed no rent figure — benchmark occupancy applied so the line above isn't overstated

buildup.label: − Labor
buildup.note: conventionally staffed · ≈ 0.9–1.4 FTE at ~$20/hr fully loaded

assumptions.detail: Classified as “Boutique fitness / studio” from the FDD (Item 1 / brand description).
Item 7 disclosed no rent — occupancy estimated at the 12–20% category band.
Estimated at the 3–10% category band — COGS is never disclosed in an FDD.
Estimated at the 20–30% conventionally staffed band — labor is never disclosed in an FDD.
Utilities, insurance, and R&M estimated at 6–10% (category catch-all).
Our calculation — the disclosed top line minus the benchmarked costs above. Not a figure stated in the FDD.

insights.consultCtaPitch: These are category benchmarks applied to the disclosed top line — your unit's real costs will differ, and that difference is the conversation. Want them pressure-tested against actual franchisee P&Ls for your territory?
insights.asOf: 2026 — general industry benchmarks, refine against real franchisee P&Ls
insights.disclaimer: Industry benchmark ranges for your own budgeting and Item 20 questions — NOT a projection of this franchise's results, and not investment advice.

financialCondition.headline: Several stress signals show up together here — a net-worth deficit of roughly $1.28M alongside operating losses, thin near-term liquidity, and unit closures. Any one of these alone can be normal for a young brand; appearing together is the pattern that separates real distress from ordinary early-stage losses. Treat this as a franchisor-stability question to resolve — with the specific items below — before you commit.
body: What we found (2025-12-31, audited by DA Advisory Group PLLC): Net loss of $673K on $360K of revenue — roughly 2.9x spent per $1 earned. Losses are widening, not shrinking. Negative net worth (a members'/stockholders' deficit) of $1.28M, and deepening year over year. Total liabilities are about 2.7x total assets. Cash of $59K against $320K of obligations due within a year (a current ratio of 0.61). Note: about 81% of those liabilities is deferred revenue — prepaid franchise fees the company owes as future services, not borrowed money. Netting it out, liabilities are still about 0.5x assets.
Why it matters to you: field support, training, technology, and marketing come out of the franchisor's budget. If outside funding slows, those are the first things to get cut.

Verify before you commit: ask for the most recent interim (unaudited) financials and the current-year funding plan; ask whether the parent will sign a guarantee of performance; and cross-check Item 20 — unit closures, transfers, and terminations are the operational tell that corroborates financial stress.
aggravators: negative net worth that is deepening year over year
more due within a year than current assets to cover it
mitigants: the auditor issued a clean (unmodified) opinion and did not raise going-concern doubt
financialCondition.evidenceNote: Flagged on the FDD's "Special Risks" page and grounded in the Item 21 / Exhibit F audited financials. Figures are extracted; all ratios and grading are computed. Confirm against the source statements and a CPA before relying on them.
